title: The Price of Risk: An Equity Risk Premium Monologue!
gist: Damodaran explains why he abandoned historical equity risk premiums for a forward-looking implied ERP, shows the implied ERP has barely moved (4.37% to 4.51%) despite a March 2026 war-and-oil shock even as the 10-year Treasury yield surged, and argues rising bond yields (inflation fear), not equity risk, are currently driving markets.

insights.claim: Standard historical equity risk premium methodology, applied at the start of 2026, would produce estimates ranging anywhere from 5.5% to 14.5% depending on the time period used, how averages are computed, and the choice of riskfree rate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A method that can output anywhere from 5.5% to 14.5% is not a reliable discount-rate input; treat any hurdle rate built on historical ERP as arbitrary unless the underlying assumptions are disclosed.

insights.claim: Historical equity risk premiums are backward-looking, statistically noisy, and easily biased because bullish analysts can select lower historical premiums while bearish analysts select higher ones to fit a predetermined conclusion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When a valuation cites a historical ERP, check which window/averaging convention was chosen — it is often a tell for the analyst's directional bias, not a neutral input.

insights.claim: Historical equity risk premiums move in the perverse direction relative to actual risk: they fall during crises (as recent poor returns get folded into the average) and rise during good times, the opposite of how a rational price of risk should behave.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discount rates built on trailing historical ERP will understate required returns exactly when risk is rising (crises) and overstate them in calm markets — a built-in procyclical error.

insights.claim: The lineage of the standard US historical return dataset — originally Ibbotson Associates, later absorbed into Duff & Phelps' cost-of-capital publications, now owned by Kroll — has been repeatedly re-sliced by industry for decades without producing much new insight, because the underlying noise in the data can't be mined away.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Be skeptical of vendor cost-of-capital products that claim finer historical-ERP breakdowns (by size, sector, style) — the source data's noise limits how much real signal can be extracted.

insights.claim: For investors who still want to use historical equity risk premiums, Damodaran recommends the Credit Suisse Yearbook over US-centric datasets because it covers roughly 20 markets over more than a hundred years and avoids the selection bias of focusing solely on US data.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If historical ERP must be used, prefer multi-country long-run datasets over single-market US series to reduce survivorship/selection bias.

insights.claim: Damodaran's forward-looking implied equity risk premium is derived by backing out the expected return (internal rate of return) markets are pricing into stocks, given current prices and expected cash flows — a model-agnostic measure of what the market is pricing in right now.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use implied ERP rather than trailing averages as the discount-rate input for valuations; it reflects current market pricing rather than a stale historical average.

insights.claim: The implied equity risk premium for US stocks at the start of 2026 was 4.23% over the treasury bond rate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete, current benchmark (4.23%) against which to sanity-check discount rates used in DCFs on US equities entering 2026.

insights.claim: Since September 2008, Damodaran shifted from computing the implied equity risk premium once a year to recomputing it at the start of every month for the S&P 500, publishing the updates on his website, precisely because he found the ERP to be a dynamic, shifting number that a single annual estimate cannot capture.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors valuing companies should refresh the discount rate at least monthly rather than locking in a start-of-year ERP, especially in volatile macro regimes.

themes.theme: ERP as a real-time crisis barometer

insights.claim: During the 2008 financial crisis, the daily-tracked implied ERP started at 4.2% on September 12, 2008 and almost doubled over the following 14 weeks as stocks went into free fall and fear about banks and the economy rose.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A near-doubling of implied ERP in ~14 weeks is the historical template for how fast discount rates should reprice in a full-blown financial crisis — a reference point for calibrating stress scenarios.
insights.quote: the crisis started with the equity risk premiums at 4.2% on September 12, 2008... but almost doubled over the next two months, as stocks went into free fall

insights.claim: Damodaran has since repeated the practice of tracking implied ERP daily during subsequent market stress episodes: the 2016 Brexit vote in the UK, the 2020 COVID-19 shutdown, the tariff-driven market turmoil of 2025, and now the March 2026 war-and-oil-price shock.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: His daily ERP series (available on his site) is a reusable, real-time gauge of market fear during any future crisis, not just a one-off 2008 exercise.

insights.claim: During the two weeks from February 27 to March 13, 2026, amid a Middle East war and oil above $100/barrel with the S&P 500 falling, the implied equity risk premium rose only modestly, from 4.37% to 4.51%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A sub-15bp rise in ERP despite a war and $100+ oil signals equity markets are not yet pricing this as a genuine crisis — a gap investors can monitor for a repricing if the conflict escalates.

insights.claim: Over the same Feb 27-Mar 13, 2026 window, the 10-year US Treasury yield rose more sharply than the equity risk premium, from 3.97% to 4.28%, indicating that inflation fears — not equity risk — are currently the dominant force moving markets.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch the bond market, not the equity risk premium, as the leading indicator of this shock's market impact; a bigger move in yields than in ERP implies the trade is about inflation/rates, not equity derating.
insights.quote: it is the ten-year US treasury bond that has had the bigger surge, up from 3.97%... to 4.28%... indicating inflation fears are trumping other market concerns right now

insights.claim: Using a fixed equity risk premium (e.g., 4%) instead of the current implied premium (e.g., 6% during a crisis) turns every company valuation into an implicit joint bet that the market itself is mispriced, rather than a judgment about the company being valued.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Before calling a stock undervalued off a DCF, check whether the analyst used a stale ERP — the 'undervaluation' may really be a hidden bet that the whole market is cheap.
insights.quote: sticking with a 4% equity risk premium during a crisis, when the implied risk premium has surged to 6% will lead you to find most companies to be undervalued, almost entirely because you think that the market is undervalued (not the company)

insights.claim: A properly market-neutral company valuation requires using the current implied equity risk premium, isolating the analyst's view on the company from any view on whether the broader market is over- or under-priced.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Adopt current implied ERP as a discipline to separate stock-picking skill from macro market-timing calls in any DCF-based process.

insights.claim: Every debate about whether the overall market is overpriced or underpriced can be reframed as a debate about whether the current implied equity risk premium is too low (overpricing), too high (underpricing), or fairly set relative to history.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use implied ERP versus its historical range as a single consolidated 'is the market cheap or expensive' metric instead of juggling PE ratios, dividend yields, and earnings yields separately.

themes.theme: The 2025-26 'too sanguine about catastrophic risk' call

insights.claim: At the start of 2026, Damodaran concluded that while US stocks looked richly priced on conventional metrics (high PE ratios, low dividend yields), the implied equity risk premium was actually in line with what US stocks have generated on average over the last 65 years — i.e., the market was not obviously overpriced through the ERP lens.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: PE ratios and dividend yields alone overstate how 'expensive' the market looks; the ERP-based read at start of 2026 argued against a market that was priced for a bubble.
insights.locator: My conclusion, at the start of 2026

insights.claim: Despite that reassuring ERP reading, Damodaran flagged 2025 as tumultuous — tariffs dominating headlines and the post-war dollar-centric global economic system starting to fray — and argued the market seemed too sanguine about catastrophic (tail) risk; weeks later, a Middle East war broke out and US equities and bonds struggled to price the resulting oil-price shock.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A market whose implied ERP looks 'normal' on average can still be underpricing tail/geopolitical risk specifically; investors should separately stress-test for catastrophic scenarios even when average-case ERP looks fine.
insights.quote: the market seems to be too sanguine about catastrophic risk
insights.locator: My conclusion, at the start of 2026

themes.theme: Small-cap premium is a zombie factor

insights.claim: The small-cap premium, a widely used valuation add-on derived from mining historical US return data (1926-2025), has not only been statistically noisy over the full period but has effectively disappeared entirely since 1981.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts still tacking a small-cap premium onto discount rates for small companies are applying an effect with roughly 45 years of no supporting evidence; challenge or drop this adjustment.
insights.locator: Historical Equity Risk Premiums (and spin offs)

insights.claim: The continued use of the small-cap premium in practice despite the evidence against it is, in Damodaran's view, a textbook case of how once a bad practice becomes embedded in valuation, it never leaves.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat entrenched valuation conventions (premiums, rules of thumb) as sticky by default regardless of evidence — inertia, not efficacy, often explains their survival.
insights.quote: once bad practices become embedded in valuation, they never leave
insights.locator: Historical Equity Risk Premiums (and spin offs)

themes.theme: Which ERP measure actually predicts returns

insights.claim: Testing the predictive efficacy of different ERP measures against actual subsequent stock returns, the implied equity risk premium shows the strongest positive correlation with returns over 5-year and 10-year horizons, while the historical risk premium performs worst, correlating in the wrong direction (actual returns rise when historical ERP falls, and vice versa).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For long-horizon return forecasting or strategic asset allocation, weight implied ERP over historical ERP as the input signal.

insights.claim: None of the equity risk premium approaches — implied or historical — forecasts next year's actual stock market return well, and even at 5-year and 10-year horizons the prediction errors remain significant.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors trying to time markets over a 1-year horizon using ERP signals are working with a tool that has essentially no short-term predictive power; don't overweight ERP-based short-term market-timing calls.
